one_line_takeaway: Stage-level 'kill-chain canary' tracking across 950 runs shows prompt-injection safety is architecture-, surface-, and role-dependent rather than a fixed model property -- e.g., Claude blocks injections when writing to shared memory (0/164 text-surface ASR) but propagates them 67% of the time when reading memory poisoned by another model, and DeepSeek swings from 0% to 100% ASR purely by attack channel.
key_finding: Exposure is universal (100% of all 764 attacked text-surface runs reach EXPOSED for every model), but downstream propagation diverges sharply: overall no-defense ASR (Table 1) is GPT-4o-mini 53% (95% CI 41-65%, n=60), DeepSeek Chat 25% (16-37%, n=68), GPT-5-mini 3% (1-7%, n=136), Claude Haiku 0% (0-5%, n=80), Claude Sonnet 0% (0-4%, n=84). In the propagation scenario (Table 2), Claude (both variants, 0/40 combined) eliminates the canary specifically at the write_memory step -- PERSISTED=0% while EXPOSED=100% -- localizing its defense to the summarization/write stage rather than to overall capability or to execution-time refusal. Critically, this defense does NOT transfer to the read position: in the Phase 3 cross-model relay (Block B, Table 6, n=3/cell), when Claude Haiku is Agent A (writer) PER=0%, but when Claude Haiku is Agent B (reader of pre-poisoned memory written by GPT-4o-mini) it propagates at EXE=67% (95% CI 21-94%) -- the paper's central architectural claim is a write-vs-read defense asymmetry, not blanket Claude immunity. DeepSeek exhibits a 0%/100% surface split (Table 3): 0/24 on memory_poison (replicated across three independent batches spanning 17 days) versus 8/8 on both tool_poison and propagation -- the same model scores as either fully safe or fully compromised depending solely on which channel is evaluated. All four active defenses fail on at least one surface via 'channel mismatch' rather than adversarial adaptation: write_filter is bypassed entirely by memory_poison because seed() pre-loads memory before the interceptor runs; pi_detector scans outgoing queries but PDF/tool-stream injections arrive in incoming content; spotlighting reduces GPT-4o-mini task success from 63% to 50% without reducing ASR at all. write_filter is the one defense the paper shows blocking at the correct stage across both text and PDF surfaces in Phase 3 (0% EXE universally, e.g. 0/3 DeepSeek pdf_append, 0/6 GPT-4o-mini pdf_whitefont). Invisible whitefont PDF payloads match or exceed visible pdf_append ASR (GPT-4o-mini: 33% whitefont vs 0% append; DeepSeek: 100%/100%), because pypdf extracts the whitefont text first, placing it before the benign abstract in extraction order -- meaning defenses that only screen the rendered/displayed document miss this vector. Objective-drift-based detection is shown to be forensic, not preventive: a GBT classifier reaches AUC=0.853 in-distribution 5-fold CV, but this collapses to AUC=0.39-0.57 (near chance) under leave-one-scenario-out evaluation, and the dominant feature (drift-after-exposure, 32.3% importance) spikes concurrently with the harmful action rather than before it. Provenance reconstruction on 22 compromised runs achieved 22/22 correct attribution with 0 false positives. GPT-5-mini failed to invoke parse_pdf in any of 18 Phase 3 runs (a tool-calling regression, 0% utility), so its Phase 3 cells are excluded from multimodal analysis. Audio transcription and PDF-metadata injection pilots (n=4 per cell) both produced 0% ASR across tested models, treated by the authors as a null result at small sample size rather than a settled finding.
core_contribution: The paper introduces a 'kill-chain canary' methodology for evaluating prompt injection against multi-agent LLM pipelines: instead of reporting a single outcome-level attack success rate (ASR), it tags each injected payload with a unique cryptographic token (SECRET-[A-F0-9]{8}) and tracks the furthest pipeline stage the token reaches — EXPOSED (appears in any tool result) -> PERSISTED (written via write_memory) -> RELAYED (read by a second agent) -> EXECUTED (appears in an outbound tool argument, i.e. ASR). The authors build a custom ~600-line harness (agent_bench) with a MemoryStore, ToolRegistry, MultiAgentOrchestrator, and PropagationLogger, and run 950 total trials (764 text-surface runs across 9 batches March 10-27, 2026, plus 186 Phase-3 multimodal runs on April 1, 2026) against five frontier models (GPT-4o-mini, GPT-5-mini, DeepSeek Chat v3, Claude Haiku 4.5, Claude Sonnet 4.5), six attack surfaces (web/tool-stream text, pre-seeded memory, PDF append, invisible whitefont PDF, PDF metadata, audio transcript), and five defense conditions (none, write_filter, pi_detector, spotlighting, all-combined). The central empirical claim is that prompt injection resilience is a pipeline-architecture property, not a monolithic model-capability property: every model is 100% exposed to every injection, but where in the pipeline the injection is neutralized (or not) differs sharply by model, by attack surface, and even by which role (writer vs. reader) a given model occupies in a multi-agent relay.
threat_model: Explicitly stated in Sec 3.2: a read-only adversary who controls content an agent will process (a PDF document, web page, or database record) but has no access to model weights, system prompts, or the agent runtime. The adversary's goal is to cause the agent to issue a specific unauthorized outbound action (send_report to an attacker-controlled address) while the legitimate task still succeeds (the 'dual-completion' pattern, citing Greshake et al.). Payloads are explicit and unobfuscated by design -- the paper states obfuscation is treated as orthogonal to the stage-localization question it is studying. Agent A and Agent B in the multi-agent scenarios communicate only through a shared MemoryStore; neither has direct access to the other's internal state.
limitations: The paper's own Sec 7 states these explicitly: (1) Sample size -- per-cell n ranges from 3 (Phase 3) to 36 (text-surface); Phase 3 cells at n=3 have wide Wilson CIs (e.g. 21-94% for the 67% Block B relay result) and are labeled pilot-scale hypotheses, not settled findings. (2) Single task -- all scenarios use one task type (document summarization + email delivery), which the authors call the primary threat to external validity. (3) Canary detection confound -- a model quoting the canary token inside a refusal explanation could be mislabeled EXECUTED; the authors manually inspected all 22 compromised text-surface runs and found no such false positives but note the confound is not systematically controlled. (4) Payloads are explicit/unobfuscated, not reflecting real-world social engineering, encoding, or multi-hop indirection (authors argue this makes their surface-mismatch results conservative, i.e. likely understating failure). (5) Only five frontier models from three providers were tested; reasoning models (o3, o4-mini) and open-weight models (Llama 3.3, Mistral) are not evaluated. (6) Defenses are lightweight wrapper approximations of the named mechanisms, not necessarily production-grade implementations. (7) The authors explicitly do not isolate whether Claude's write-stage filtering arises from training data, system prompt, or tool-API design, calling this an open ablation question. (8) The near-zero permission_esc ASR (2/132) is attributed by the authors themselves to weak/overt payload design rather than genuine privilege-escalation resistance. (9) GPT-5-mini's 0% Phase 3 tool-call rate could not be diagnosed as capability regression vs. API/schema incompatibility from black-box evaluation alone.

datasets_used: agent_bench (self-built ~600-line multi-agent evaluation harness; not an external dataset)

950 self-generated evaluation runs: 764 text-surface runs (428 no-defense attacked, 80 with-defense attacked, 256 clean controls) + 186 Phase 3 multimodal runs (138 homogeneous-pair Block A + 48 cross-model Block B, n=3/cell)
quantitative_results: Overall no-defense ASR (Table 1): GPT-4o-mini 53% (95% CI 41-65%, n=60); DeepSeek Chat 25% (16-37%, n=68); GPT-5-mini 3% (1-7%, n=136); Claude Haiku 0% (0-5%, n=80); Claude Sonnet 0% (0-4%, n=84)
Exposure rate = 100% for all models across all 764 attacked text-surface runs
Propagation scenario stage fractions (Table 2): GPT-4o-mini and DeepSeek 100%/100%/100% at PERSISTED/RELAYED/EXECUTED; GPT-5-mini 15%/15%/15%; Claude Haiku and Claude Sonnet 0%/0%/0%
DeepSeek surface split (Table 3): 0/24 on memory_poison vs 8/8 on tool_poison and 8/8 on propagation
Claude aggregate: 0/164 ASR across all four text-surface scenarios, no defenses

Phase 3 Block A (Table 5, n=3/cell): DeepSeek 100% EXE for both pdf_append and pdf_whitefont; Claude Haiku and Sonnet 0% PER (and thus 0% EXE) for both variants; GPT-4o-mini 0% EXE (pdf_append) vs 33% EXE (pdf_whitefont); GPT-5-mini 0% EXP (tool-calling regression, excluded from further analysis)

Phase 3 Block B cross-model relay (Table 6, n=3/cell): Claude Haiku as Agent A -> Agent B GPT-4o-mini gives PER/REL/EXE = 0%/0%/0% (CI 0-56%); Claude Haiku as Agent B (writer GPT-4o-mini) gives EXE=67% (CI 21-94%); GPT-4o-mini->DeepSeek and DeepSeek->GPT-4o-mini pairs both reach 100% EXE (CI 44-100%)
write_filter defense: 0% EXE universally in Phase 3 (0/3 DeepSeek pdf_append, 0/3 DeepSeek pdf_whitefont, 0/6 GPT-4o-mini pdf_whitefont)
pi_detector on PDF surface: fails completely -- DeepSeek pdf_append remains 100% EXE, GPT-4o-mini pdf_whitefont remains 33% EXE, identical to no-defense
Spotlighting: reduces GPT-4o-mini attacked-task success from 63% to 50% with no reduction in ASR

Objective-drift classifier: AUC=0.853 in-distribution (5-fold CV, 21 features) collapsing to AUC=0.39-0.57 under leave-one-scenario-out; drift_mean_after_exposure feature accounts for 32.3% of importance (3x next feature)
Provenance attribution heuristic: 22/22 (100%) compromised runs correctly traced, 0 false attributions
permission_esc ASR: 2/132 total (DeepSeek 1/28, GPT-5-mini 1/36), attributed by authors to weak payload design
Execution lag: GPT-4o-mini median 1 step (mean 1.25, max 2); DeepSeek bimodal (most within 2-3 steps, tail up to 12 steps); GPT-5-mini mean 3.0 steps when compromised
Audio and PDF-metadata injection pilots: 0% ASR across all tested model/surface combinations (n=4 per cell)
baselines_compared: Outcome-only ASR measurement (the standard single-metric evaluation the paper argues is insufficient, contrasted throughout with its own stage-level decomposition)
Five defense conditions compared against a no-defense control: none, write_filter, pi_detector, spotlighting, and all three combined
In-distribution 5-fold CV vs. leave-one-scenario-out evaluation of the same GBT trajectory classifier (used as an internal ablation to show the drift signal does not generalize)
